science.title: Spatio-temporal metabarcoding surveys in ports reveal homogenised communities of non-indigenous species with high genetic diversity and connectivity
science.description: Large commercial ports facilitate the introduction of non-indigenous species (NIS), while smaller harbours and marinas promote their regional spread, making harbour networks key drivers of biological invasions. We analysed spatio-temporal patterns of metazoan communities over one year in four medium-size harbours, as well as at an outside reference point, along the NW Mediterranean coast using standardised biological collectors and COI metabarcoding. We identified 1,774 metazoan molecular operational taxonomic units (MOTUs), of which 75 were classified as NIS. Although less diverse than native species, NIS accounted for 34-70% of reads in harbours, with the southernmost harbour showing the highest abundance, likely due to proximity to aquaculture facilities. Spatial structure varied among harbours, but NIS showed consistently low genetic differentiation and shared more MOTUs among sites than native species. Seasonal patterns affected both NIS and native communities. NIS also exhibited higher haplotype diversity and lower genetic differentiation across harbours, suggesting spread via local boating and recurrent introductions. These results highlight contrasting dynamics between NIS and native species in artificial environments and underscore the need for continued monitoring of harbour networks.

science.title: The Universe is Bending Light, and Astronomers Need Your Help to Find it
science.description: Einstein told us that massive objects bend light and he was of course, right. Across the universe, giant galaxies are acting as natural telescopes, warping and distorting the light of objects behind them into spectacular arcs and rings. Now the Euclid space telescope wants your help to find them and the scale of the hunt is unlike anything attempted before.

science.title: Mining the Solar System to Build a New World
science.description: If humans are ever going to live permanently on Mars, someone is going to have to work out where all the raw materials, the food, they oxygen or the material for the structures to name just a few. A new study has tackled that unglamorous but absolutely critical question and the answer involves robots, asteroids, and one of the most complex supply chains ever designed.

science.title: The Planet Haul That Changes Everything.
science.description: NASA's planet hunting telescope has been busy. A new study has just sifted through the light of over 83 million stars and emerged with more than 11,000 potential worlds, including a confirmed giant planet orbiting a distant star. The results don't just add to our catalogue of planets. They fundamentally change where we look for them.

science.title: Effect of a combined thermal protection strategy with plantar hot salt packs on preventing intraoperative hypothermia during knee arthroscopy: a randomized controlled trial
science.description: This study aimed to evaluate the efficacy of a combined thermal protection strategy incorporating plantar hot salt packs for preventing intraoperative hypothermia in patients undergoing knee arthroscopy. This randomized controlled trial enrolled 120 patients undergoing knee arthroscopy between January 2024 and December 2024. Patients were randomly assigned to either the experimental group (n = 60), which received routine care plus plantar hot salt packs, or the control group (n = 60), which received routine thermal care alone. The primary outcome was core body temperature, recorded at the following time points: admission (T0), 15 min after anesthesia induction (T1),3 0 min after surgery initiation (T2), at the end of surgery (T3), and upon arrival in the anesthesia recovery room (T4). Secondary outcomes included the incidence of intraoperative hypothermia, postoperative chills, patient comfort, and satisfaction. After adjusting for baseline temperature (T0) using analysis of covariance (ANCOVA), the experimental group maintained significantly higher core body temperatures at T1, T2, T3, and T4 than the control group (all adjusted P < 0.001). No significant between-group differences were observed in the incidence of intraoperative hypothermia or postoperative chills (both P > 0.05). Patient comfort was significantly higher in the experimental group (P < 0.05). Although patient satisfaction was numerically higher in the experimental group (98.3% vs. 88.3%), the difference was not statistically significant (P = 0.061). A combined thermal protection strategy using plantar hot salt packs effectively maintains perioperative core body temperature and significantly improves patient comfort during knee arthroscopy. While this strategy did not significantly reduce the incidence of hypothermia or chills, it represents a safe, non-invasive, and easily implemented adjunctive warming method that may help optimize intraoperative temperature management. Trial registration: Chinese Clinical Trial Registry, ChiCTR2600120304 (registered March 12, 2025).
